steps.title: Identify the relative clause boundary
steps.content: The blank comes between “an ecological” and the clause “compared the lengths of eggs produced by different bird species found Calidris alpina eggs to be longer, on average, than Oriolus xanthonotus eggs.” This clause modifies “study,” and the grammar depends on whether it's restrictive (essential to identifying the noun) or non-restrictive (additional information).

steps.title: Apply the restrictive clause rule
steps.content: Restrictive relative clauses, which provide essential information, use that with no comma: “study that compared...” Non-restrictive clauses, which provide extra information, use which with a comma: “study, which compared...” Here, the clause defines what the study did and found—essential information for understanding its purpose. Use that without punctuation. Option D violates the rule by placing a comma before that; option C treats essential information as non-restrictive; option B eliminates the relative pronoun, creating an awkward structure. That matches choice A — A is correct.
